data.content:
Your project is already set up for hot reload. Here's how to use it:

**Run tests in hot-reload mode:**

```bash
dotnet test -- --hotreload
```

This keeps the test host running. When you save changes to your `.cs` files, it automatically re-applies them via .NET Hot Reload and re-runs the tests — no full rebuild needed.

**Your current issue:** `IsValidEmail` only checks for non-empty strings but doesn't check for `@`, so `Validate_MissingAtSign_ReturnsFalse` will fail. With hot reload running, you can fix that method and see the tests re-run instantly.

For example, fix line 27 to:

```csharp
return !string.IsNullOrEmpty(email) && email.Contains('@');
```

Save the file and the tests will automatically re-execute. Want me to start the hot-reload session and fix the tests?
data.reasoningText:
The user wants to know how to use `Microsoft.Testing.Extensions.HotReload` to iterate on test fixes. Let me explain the workflow.

The HotReload extension allows you to run tests in a "watch" mode where you can edit code, and the tests will automatically re-run without a full rebuild. This is similar to `dotnet watch` but specifically for the testing platform.

The way to use it is with `dotnet test --hotreload` or by running the test app directly with `--hotreload`.

Let me check if I know the exact command. With the Microsoft Testing Platform (which this project uses since it has `TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport`), you can use:


You run your tests with the `--hotreload` flag, and the testing platform keeps the test host running while watching for file changes. When you save code, .NET Hot Reload applies the changes and automatically re-runs the tests without needing a full rebuild. The command is `dotnet test -- --hotreload` or you can run the test app directly with the same flag.
